sentences.text: I got a potato and a glass of water.
I came to me again.
You know, I felt better.
So we went into the barn, you know, to stay overnight.
And the barn was like wheat, corn or something.
And you see on-- on top, you know, like you make bread, you know, from what-- from wheat.
And we used to get the kernels.
I believe that's what you call it-- the kernels-- have to rub it out.
You know.
And we used to eat  those kernels, you know, from the barn-- from the-- from, you know, in the barn.
And the next morning, again, the same thing.
We went on the march.
Then they put us on-- in box cars, like cattle trains.
You know.
They put us in those box cars on the way to-- to Buchenwald.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, yeah.
They put us in-- they packed us in like-- like sardines.
I remember a German, you know, went in-- went on top in the train, you know.
And he looked in to see how much room there is or something.
He used to take the-- the end of the-- of the gun.
You know.
They used to push, you know, push in more and more.
I mean, standing room only.
You know, you couldn't lay down.
And it was full, tight.
They didn't-- they locked the door, closed the door, no air, no nothing.
No.
No windows, no nothing, you know, just-- just box cars.
And they stacked us in.
And then after a while, some-- some guy in front of me, he died, right down-- fall down.
I stood on top of his body so this way gave me more-- more-- more air to breathe.
I had more room to breath.
Yeah.
I stood on top of the body.
And I don't know how long we were in that-- you know, in those box cars and everything.
But we came from Buchenwald.
When they opened, sliding doors opened, there was people falling out.
You know, and I get out.
You know, I don't know how I made it.
I don't know.
You know.
Oh, yeah, suffocated.
Yeah.
I stood on top on a body, you know, so this way-- you know, and came out from the train.
And I passed where the locomotive is, you know, from the trains get their water, you know, fill their water up.
And passed by, and I grabbed my-- you know, I went up there, you know, grabbed the hand in the water, and put some on me, put on me water.
And I feel better again.
You know, I almost died.
And we went inside the camps.
And I remember it was so hot in the room that was fainted.
And a guy walked around with a bowl of cold water, you know, sprinkle water on me, and same thing, grabbed the water, went back again.
And I was assigned to a room, you know, in Buchenwald.
No.
No, just went into the camp.
And I was assigned a room.
And the same thing, a piece of bread, you know, and soup.
And then they had stone pit.
It was a big pit, you know, stones, rocks.
And that's how they-- they-- they put me in to keep me occupied, that I work in the pits.
I took the rocks, you know, from this side, you know, in the pit, put them on this side.
You know.
And there was no more rocks on this side-- it was filled up-- I took the rocks from here, put them over here.
You know, and that's how I stayed in Buchenwald.
I worked till, you know.
No.
No.
In Buchenwald, I got a new number, 128923.
That was my Buchenwald number.
No.
No.
I just had it on my uniform, not tattooed.
You know, that's on my uniform, 128923.
And I worked there, and then, you know, getting weaker, and weaker, and weaker.
And-- Then we hear the shooting, you know, planes flying all over the place.
And then in the last few days, they start taking out again, you know, the Germans, the people from the-- from the people to go on the march.
And I was weaker and weaker.
And, you know, I couldn't do it.
So one day the German come in.
He said, [GERMAN].
You know, it means out.
You know, and they put us on the march.
And I figured, no.
I-- I can't go.
So I went out.
And in front of the barracks, when somebody died in the barracks, they took the body, put it in the front by the-- by the door.

And then a little wagon with a couple guys walked by, you know, a little open wagon, and threw the bodies on top on the wagon, you know, to-- to take in the furnace, you know, in the crematorium, you know, to-- to burn you.
So when it took me, I went out in the barracks, and I fell down on those dead once, you know, laid on the dead ones.
And you know, he took other guys out, the German, you know, with his pistol in his hand, you know, and everything.
And when he walked out from the barracks, he thought I was one of the dead ones.
So, you know, he just left me there.
And when he went away, I walked back into the barrack.
And same token, you know, we had a Polish couple in there.
I mean, he was a miserable guy, you know, especially, you know, Jews, you know.
And he was mad.
And he said, you're supposed to be on the march.
What are you doing here?
You know.
I'm going to report you to the-- to the-- to the German, you know, that you're still here.
You're supposed to be there.
And the same thing, I said, look.
I said, I can't walk no more.
I can do nothing.
Let me die here.
I mean, I was like this.
You know.
Let me die here.
I give you my ration, the piece of bread I'll get, you know, tomorrow, today, from my thing, I'll give it to you, I said.
I don't want it.
I want to die here.
Leave me die here in the camp.
Well, he didn't-- he didn't went and tell him.
You know.
And the next couple days, back again, the German, you know, to take out more people on the march.
I figure if I go out on the march, I can't walk.
It's the same thing.
You know.
I fall down.
I might walk a few feet.
You know, the German go out there, take a pistol, and put on my head, shoot me, you know, and then put me on the side.
And I was thinking, I said, I'd rather die here in the camp here.
If I die, they throw me in a furnace, burn me, and that's it here.
If I die outside, I said, the dogs in the village, I had in my mind might chew me up, you know, and eat me up.
I said, you know-- I said, I'd rather die here.
And came again.
You know, and I couldn't walk.
I lay on the bunk, on the thing.
And came up to me.
And I crawled out.
You know.
I lay down on the middle the floor.
And the German put the pistol to my head.
And he said, better get up, if not, I'll shoot you.
You know.
And he said, like in German, [GERMAN].
You know.
Anyways, he said, I'm not going to waste a bullet on you.
[GERMAN] mean you're dropping dead anyways.
[GERMAN].
He said, I'm not gonna waste a bullet on you.
You see.
And he left me.
He went to get somebody else.
And I crawled back in the-- you know, to my bunk.
You know.
And the next day, I was liberated.
And [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I heard the guys come in running.
They said, we're free.
We're free.
You know.
I couldn't believe it.
[CRYING] So I crawled out from my bunk.
And hauled down.
And I went to the door, you know, opened up.
And I-- and I-- I saw one of our prisoners, you know, had a German, the German who watched the towers five minutes ago in the [INAUDIBLE].
They got him down from the tower.
They had his gun.
You know, he's wearing his this on the top of the head, and this [INAUDIBLE].
I couldn't believe it.
That was-- was a sight to see it.
And I got freed.
And--
The American-- American Army came in and disarming the camp.
Mind you, disarming the camp, and they were still watching of us, the Germans.
You know, we shouldn't-- shouldn't run away.
And they came in.
I couldn't walk no more.
So maybe a day, two days later, you know, they came and picked me up and took me in an ambulance.
I couldn't even get up on top on the Red Cross.
They had that army, you know, ambulance with the cross on it, Red Cross.
And it had a little step.
And I couldn't make the step up to go into the ambulance.
Lift me up, took me in, and-- and took me in an ambulance.
And then they took me into a hospital in-- to the city.
You know.
And I stayed there in the hospital.
And I remember American doctors came in.
And they, you know, asked me all kind of questions.
And they starting us to feed, you know, liquid, you know, little by little.
Lots of people died, you know, had typhus.
And other people died, was, you know, another thing.
And I remember the beds were all full.
You know.

So they gave me a room, put on blankets, maybe four or five blankets, Army blankets, you know, on the floor, a couple blankets to cover yourself, a couple blankets to make a pillow, you know, and we lay there.
And as I said, you know, they fed us, gave me blood, and whatever I needed, and fed us liquid, you know, little by little to get to-- and-- I made it.
And I don't know how long I stayed in the hospital.
Then they came, and-- the Americans.
And they said, well, you have two choices.

One choice is-- is that you stay here, you'll be-- the Russians take over, because somehow-- I don't know why-- they get one part the Russians gave to the United States, one part the United States gave to Russians.
This part here would be to the Russians.
So you have a choice to stay here.
The Russians will take over.
Or, you want to go with us, you know, we will take you someplace else to our zone, you know, American zone, or the Russians.
So I said, well, I figure I have family in-- in the United States.
You know.
And I don't know what happened to my family.
You know.
I always get a change to go back.
I'd be better off to go, you know, with Americans.
So I left with Americans.
You know.
Then I went to a DP camp.
It was Landsberg, Landsberg am Lech.
You know.
And I went to DP camp.
And I got stronger already.
You know, I feel OK.
And in that camp, I stayed there, didn't do too much, tried to teach me a trade.
And it was ORT, you know, came in.
You know the Jewish organization, ORT, and tried to teach me a trade.
I remember I worked machines, you know, and the other thing.
And I waited about four years in this camp, I was to came to the United States.
Well, after I was in DP camps.

And one of my friends, one of my hometown guys, he-- when they gathered all the Jews, you know, in my hometown after I left-- I was in the other camp-- they gathered the Jews, my father, mother, all the families, and put them in the church.
And from there, they shipped them out to-- to Auschwitz.
My sisters, you know, my family, they shipped them out to-- to Auschwitz.
He's the one who escaped, or he wasn't there at that time.
He's the one that come.
He told me, you know, they were shipped out to Auschwitz, you know, and everything, and what happened.
All my parents, you know, my sisters, whole family, I'm the only one-- I made it-- you know, perished in Auschwitz.
Well, from my family, I'm the only one.
Nobody.
Not nobody.
I'm the only one.
And I had an uncle and aunt in Denmark and cousins.
And I had an uncle here in the United States, you know, cousins, and the other thing, which they left for Poland, the First World War, before First World War.
That's where they stayed.

Now, when I went, and they took me away from my parents, you know, when they took me into camps, my mother gave me two addresses to take with me, address from my uncle from the United States, and address in Denmark.
And I had those addresses up to Auschwitz.
You know, in the civilian camps-- in civilian camps, when I was there, I could keep, you know, things with me, pictures, or anything.
You know.
But when I came to Auschwitz, you know, I was stripped of everything.
And they gave you that uniform, that stripped uniform, that was it.
So when I was in-- in the DP camp after the war, you know, people start finding out, and writing, you know, I'll see you, and another thing.
And-- and I believe Red Cross came in our camp and wanted to know, you know, we have any relatives some place, you know.
And, you know, they send away information.
And I remember the-- the address from Denmark.
I remembered it very well.
I remembered the whole address and everything.
I gave it to him.
From-- from the United States, I remember my uncle's name.
But I know he changed it.
My uncle used to be Isaac.
He changed it to Cohn.
I remember that.
And I remember the city.
And I know he was a baker, but that's it.
So I gave him all the information.
In Detroit, yeah, the city of Detroit.
And I gave him the information.
And if you want to know if you have any letters or any information, they had in the camp-- you know we had our Jewish guys around the camp-- we had a bulletin board.
And you went there every day, you know, to check on the bulletin board, if you see your name.
And that's all I knew from my uncle is I didn't have all the information, which I gave it to him.
And-- and I know he was a baker.
So well, they figured, you know, they know the city of Detroit.
They figure he's a baker.
He must belong to a union.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they called up the union.
And-- and they said, you have a Meyer Cohn.
He said, yeah.
And they said, OK.
Can we talk to him or-- or see him and everything?
He said, yeah.
And he got-- they got in touch with him.
They said, you have a-- you know, a nephew.
You know, your-- your sister's name was-- I gave them all the information.
The sister's name was my mother.
You know.
They lived in this and this city, you know.
And there's another thing, a nephew by the name of [INAUDIBLE] and everything, you know.
And he said, yeah.
I said, well, we've got the right person.
And they gave the information.
Then he sent a letter.
And he said who he was and everything.
And that's how I find out.
And I got the uncle.
And we start writing to each other.
You know, and he's the one who sponsored me here to come.
And he said, you wait.
Because I had a chance to go to Australia.
And I had a chance to go to Israel.
So I figure the same thing.
I have, you know, family here.
So I go, you know, to my uncle.
You know.
And he's the one sponsored me.
It took me about four years, you know, to get here.
Didn't have nothing.
When I was a kid, as I said, you know, I was a young kid.
I didn't know from-- from nothing.
I just went to the Polish school, learned a little bit, wherever, fifth grade, sixth grade, you know, had a cheder, you know.
And, you know, I didn't-- too much.
Just waiting to be shipped out.
Yeah.
Well, yeah.
We had, you know, activities.
The same thing-- we played soccer, you know, things, you know, and sing songs, you know, or we went to a different city, you know, and everything.
But that's it.
I came to the States in 1949.
You know.
And, you know, I didn't know for no language or nothing.
And--
Well, you mean-- I came by boat, you know, from-- from Germany.
I believe it was Bremerhaven.
It took me about 11 days to get here.
And 10 days I was sick, you know, on the boat, throwing up your guts, you know, from the-- And then I came to New York.
And by-- and some lady picked us up, I believe from HIAS-- HIAS and gave me $10.

And another friend of mine too, the same thing when we came, and gave us $10 in New York to get-- and I remember, when we came to New York-- yeah, that's right-- she asked me when she picked us up from the boat, she asked us if we were hungry.
I said, yeah.
OK.
So, you know, said we'll eat.
So they had stands.
[INAUDIBLE].
And they said, would you like a hot dog?
I said, oh, Lord.
I said, a hot dog.
A hot dog, heiss Hund, you know, a dog.
I said, I didn't eat no dogs in the camps.
I said, I'm not gonna eat no dogs here.
[LAUGHS] You know, and I said, I'm not gonna eat no hot dog and everything.
And he said, no.
You see, take a look what it is.
And I looked.
It looked like a little salami.
You know.
I said, OK.
You know.
I get.
You know.
So I got the-- and-- you know, I came-- came here by train from New York.
Took us a whole night.
And then my aunt and uncle, uncle Meyer and Auntie [?
Hannah, ?] you know.
Yeah.
From Detroit picked us up.
And I stayed in the house.
You know.
He said, we'll give you two weeks, you know.
And, you know.
You'll stay here, then you go look for a job.
And I stayed with them.
And then I started going to night school, you know, to learn the language.
And then I went to-- to look for a job.
And I had my aunt's-- my aunt's brother, worked at-- it was a factory in Hamtramck, which was mostly Polish people.
It was a factory, Dodge, Dodge main.
So he took me down and said, maybe I'll get a job.
So I went with him, you know, to-- to Hamtramck to work in the Dodge factory.
And I thought, well, maybe he knows somebody.
You know, he can introduce me, you know, maybe get a job.
But he dropped me off, you know, in front of the factory.
And there was a big line of people standing in line.
He said, you see this line?
I said, yes.
He said, you go in the line.
When you come up there, you ask for a job.
And I didn't know not a word, I mean, of English.
I said, oh boy.
You know.
OK.
So he dropped me off.
And then to go back here, wrote down the addresses, you know, where to go, what street car to take, where it got off.
I had landmarks, you know, where to go.
And I went into line.
When I come up to the front desk, and I say, yeah.
[INAUDIBLE].
I said, me want job.
You know.
I said, me want joy.
So he gave me a form to fill out.
And there was a big table sitting down, people, you know, writing.
You know, but I didn't know nothing about it.
So I took the form and took back the street car.
You know, I told the street car, the guy had written down piece of paper where to go, you know, and made my way back home.
And I gave it to my cousins.
They filled out, the forms.
And the next day, I dropped it off to them, you know, and everything.
Same way ?] went back.
And a couple weeks-- I don't remember how long-- they called me up.
And-- and they offered me a job.
And I worked for Dodge, you know, for a while, I don't know, a year, two years.
And I didn't care for, you know, factory work.
So I want to learn a trade.
I mean, I didn't have nothing-- you know, and learned a trade.
And the same thing, my aunt had another brother, which was in the glass business.
I said, OK.
I'll try to be a plumber, electrician, anything.
You know.
But-- So I told my boss in the factory.
I said, well, I'm going away for a couple weeks.
You know, I said I have family in California or someplace.
I didn't tell him I was looking for another job.
But they go-- so I took off.
And I figure for in case I don't like it, you know, I can always go back.
And I went.
And I see it, learned the trade, I said, OK.
It's better than work in the factory.
And, you know, I worked in the factory.
And it happened too in the factory, they had an incident with lots of Polish people worked in that factory.
Because it was a Polish town.
Hamtramck, you know, is like a-- lots of Polish population.
And when I worked there, they didn't know I was Jewish.
All those Polish people, they thought, you know, I'm a Polack.
I talk to them in Polish, you know, and everything.
And then we came-- Jewish holidays, like Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, I told my boss, you know, I'm not working tomorrow because it's the holiday.
He said, OK.
And you know.
And the next day after holidays, I came to work.
So one Polack, you know, walked up to me.
And he said, you're a god damn Jew, aren't you?
I said, that's right.
And I pulled my pants down right in front in the factory.
And I said, kiss my ass.
And kiss all Jewish ass.
And I said, if you go say one more Jew, I said, your dentist will have lots of work to do on you.
I said, don't you say nothing.
I said, if you want to call me by name, I said, you call my name.
Otherwise, don't say a word.
And he walked away.
I say, this is not Poland now, I told him.
You know.
And everything was all right.
The next day I come back.
He said, hello, Mr.
Steve.
And I said, that's fine.
I said don't call me Mr.
Steve.
Just call me Steve.
But I don't want to hear that word no more.
And never.
We-- we were buddies.
You know, he didn't say a word no more.
Well, not-- not so much.
They asked.
But I couldn't say.
I couldn't talk to, you know, you know, anything.
Not too much.
They were-- they were friendly, you know, tried to help me the best they could, you know.
But [?
I don't know, ?] problem-- the only problem I had was the language.
You know.
But I didn't know how to drive, you know, in a car, you know, and nothing.
I went to night school, learned the language.
And then I went to driver license-- I mean to driver's ed.
You know.
And then no problem.
I met my wife-- I came-- what-- in '49, maybe a couple years later.
Phyllis.
Was [?
Ziegler. ?] You know, we met on a blind date.
Yeah.
And I-- and I never let her out of the sight since then.
You know.
Married 42 years.
And I want to say one more incident I had with my son.
You know, at the time, we tried at dinner time, you know, always tried to, with the family, where the kids sit together, tried to be at home.
You know, sometimes, couldn't work out.
But working and I couldn't make it to dinner.
You know, so I wrote it down on my arm, I should remember my telephone number.
And that was it.
When he start going to school, you know, and he had homework to do, bring home, you know, he would always ask his mother, my wife, how you spell so and so this word, and you spell this word.
You know.
And he keep asking all the time-- add, you know.
Then one day, I said, Mark, how come you never ask your father, you know, how to spell your name?
He said, Dad, how can I ask you to spell your name?
You don't even remember your telephone number.
[LAUGHS] You know?
[LAUGHS] You know.
So-- Did you eventually start telling your family about--
Well, when they older, you know, they start asking.
You know.
I couldn't tell them.
You know, people ask me, schools, you know, to go and tell my life story.
I-- I can't do that.
You know.
But eventually, you know, I start telling them little by little.
You know.
I have a daughter by the name Bonnie.
I have a daughter named Lisa, you know.
Yeah, all married.
I have seven grandchildren.
You know.
I have a daughter-in-law by the name of Carol.
And I have a son-in-law named Shawn.
And I have a son-in-law named Paul.
Yeah.
And I have seven children.
I have twin girls, you know, which they're gonna be a little bit over two years old.
You know.
Yeah, well, we were observant, went to shul, you know, every-- every Saturday, and went to-- to-- to like the beis madresh, you know, twice a day in the morning.
Well, when I went to school, I couldn't make it.
You know, but in the evenings we went to the beis madresh.
You know.
And we were always busy, you know, with the religion, you know, Polish school, Jewish school, cheder.
You know.
And I remember one incident, too, where we had our rabbi in cheder.
He had a long beard.
And-- but teaching us-- well, maybe it was six, eight kids, maybe 10 of us, he used to always fall asleep.
You know, usually, you know, he'd fall asleep.
[LAUGHS] We were glad you fall asleep because this will give us time we didn't have to learn, you know.
So one time I took some glue and-- and-- and glued his beard, a long beard.
And I glued his beard to the table.
And we waited a couple minutes, you know, the glue dry up.
[LAUGHS] And then we hollered, fire, fire!
And he [INAUDIBLE].
He run out, he left half-- half of the beard on the table.
[LAUGHS] And then he woke up.
He said, who did it?
Who did it?
And nobody's going to squeal, you know, who did it.
Nobody's going to say nothing.
So he-- he beat the hell out of everybody, you know, got beaten up, and anything.
We did things, you know.
We had to do something to keep us occupied.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
I just-- I just wonder why, why I keep asking.
Why?
Well, a little bit.
I would say so.
I would say so.
I said, why he let those things happen, you know, have six million Jews, I mean, innocent people, children, you know, I mean-- even non-- non-Jewish even?
It was 10 million, I believe, Gentiles, you know, have killed.
